name: Comedy Breakdown
description: Humor-first content that dissects what makes a show, scene, or character genuinely funny. Celebrates absurd premises, running gags, character comedy, and the gap between expectation and reality. Leans into the chaos with a voice that's entertained, not just informative.

structure.hook_rule: Open with the most absurd premise or the single funniest moment — but frame it as a question: 'What happens when [ridiculous situation]?' The viewer should immediately feel like they're in on a joke. Never explain the punchline before the setup.
structure.pacing_rule: Structure around escalating absurdity — start odd, get weirder, peak at the most unhinged moment. Each beat should either subvert an expectation or pay off a setup from two beats ago. Spend one sentence max setting up, one sentence delivering the punchline. Never linger.
structure.ending_rule: End with a callback to the opening premise, but with the new context making it funnier: 'Remember that [opening setup]? Yeah, it gets worse.' Leave the viewer feeling like they just watched something they can't unsee.
structure.tone: Amused co-conspirator — you're not explaining jokes, you're sharing them with a friend who will immediately understand why it's funny. Dry wit over hype. Deadpan works. The funniest delivery is often the most matter-of-fact one.
transitions_en: And then somehow it gets worse...
But wait, it doesn't stop there...
Which brings us to the real chaos...
And obviously, that's when everything falls apart...
transitions_vi: Và rồi mọi thứ còn tệ hơn nữa...
Nhưng chờ đã, chưa hết đâu...
Điều này dẫn đến màn hỗn loạn thực sự...
Và tất nhiên, đó là lúc mọi thứ sụp đổ...

prompt_injection: FORMAT: Comedy highlight reel with escalating absurdity. CRITICAL: Every beat needs a setup AND a punchline — never explain why something is funny, just present it and let it land. Structure: absurd hook premise → first funny moment → escalating chaos → peak absurdity → callback closer. Use the character's situation against them: their logic, their failures, their unexpected consequences. Write like a friend retelling the funniest thing they saw this week, not like a critic analyzing humor. Minimum 200 words.
extraction_hint: A fact MATCHES if it highlights: a character doing something that backfires hilariously, an absurd gap between a character's self-image and reality, a situation that escalates in an unexpected direction, or a running gag with a great payoff. A fact DOES NOT MATCH if it is: a serious plot point, a dramatic moment, or a general description with no comedic element. When in doubt: ask 'would someone retell this story to get a laugh?' If no, reject it.
quality_gate_questions: HOOK: Does the first line land an absurd or unexpected premise that immediately signals this will be funny?
SURPRISE: Is there at least one moment of genuine comedic surprise — a punchline or beat the viewer didn't see coming?
SPECIFICITY: Does the humor come from specific details rather than vague descriptions that could apply to anything?
CALLBACK: Does the last line deliver a punchline or callback to the opening premise?
ENERGY: Does the energy stay consistently playful throughout — no tonal dips into dry reporting or overly serious narration?
coverage_dimensions: the bit or scene being analyzed
why it is funny (comic mechanism — timing, subversion, absurdity)
the hidden layer or darker reading most viewers miss
recurring jokes or callbacks that define the show
what makes this creator or show uniquely funny vs everyone else
